text:
## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This method book is a comprehensive, sequentially structured improvisation pedagogy that moves from the simplest harmonic building blocks to sophisticated chromatic and symmetrical scale systems. The arc follows a clear logic: Chapters 1–8 establish the major-key universe (triads, sixths, sevenths, ninths, major scales, digital patterns, intervals, and diatonic chord nomenclature); Chapters 9–17 introduce modal thinking starting with Mixolydian and Dorian, culminating in the IIm7–V7–IM7 progression as the jazz harmonic lingua franca; Chapters 18–28 expand into non-diatonic and symmetrical scale territories (whole tone, diminished, augmented, polychords, altered extensions); and Chapters 29–35 consolidate technique through fourths-based improvisation, interval studies, and chromatic integration. Each new harmonic domain is introduced conceptually, then immediately converted into transposable practice patterns, and the student is never permitted to linger in a single key — transposition to all twelve keys is a standing, non-negotiable directive throughout.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The method is built on a small set of interlocking systems that recur across every chapter. The **Chord Tone Chart** (introduced in Chapter 1) is the master reference organizing chord types by their scale-degree formulas (1-3-5 for triads; 1-3-5-7 for sevenths; 1-3-5-7-9 for ninths), with the enharmonic equivalence principle collapsing upper extensions to lower scale degrees (9=2, 11=4, 13=6). **Roman numeral notation** (Chapter 8) designates chord function by scale degree, kept strictly distinct from the arabic numerals used for scale-degree formulas. **Modal derivation** is the method's core harmonic engine: every chord quality is traced back to a parent mode — Major and Ionian for major chord families (Chapters 1–7), Mixolydian for dominant seventh and ninth chords (Chapter 9), Dorian for all minor chord types (Chapters 11–15) — and the student is expected to internalize the full family of modes for every major key (Chapter 9 mandates this as a structural gate). **Directional alternation** (Chapters 3, 14, 34) — systematically practicing patterns ascending then descending and vice versa — is the defining technical drill that prevents directional habits and builds symmetrical muscle memory. **Chord-group reading** (Chapter 16) trains the student to think in harmonic units (IIm7–V7–IM7) rather than isolated chords, and is presented as the skill that enables idiomatic jazz spontaneity.

## Rules and Prescriptions

The book establishes explicit, mandatory rules that function as pedagogical law. Slurred articulation is the default for all pattern practice, with variation permitted only to deepen learning (Chapter 1). Eighth-note notation is a baseline that must be rhythmically liberated in actual improvisation to avoid mechanical execution (Chapter 1). Minimum-tempo mastery criteria must be met before advancing (Chapter 1). Transposition across all twelve keys is never optional — it is required for adequate results on any chord type or scale (Chapters 1, 5, 13, 22, 31, 32, 33, 35). Students must write out scale-tone triads for all twelve keys before beginning pattern work in diatonic harmony (Chapter 8), and must write and internalize the full mode family for every major key before proceeding past dominant harmony (Chapter 9). The chord-duration rule (two beats per chord) governs the metrical framework for Dorian-mode arpeggio exercises (Chapter 14). The two-distinct-collections rule for whole-tone scales (Chapter 18) and the three-distinct-collections rule for diminished scales (Chapter 27) and four-collections rule for augmented scales (Chapter 28) establish economy-of-pitch principles students must apply rather than re-deriving. Pattern work on diminished scales must cover every transposition root shown in the reference chart (Chapter 22), and augmented-triad transposition requires only three roots (Bb, C, Db) while diminished sevenths require additional roots to complete their cycle (Chapter 35).

## Explicit Methodology

The method's pedagogical architecture rests on four pillars. First, **formulaic extraction**: every chord is derived by extracting numbered scale tones from a named parent mode — the student never memorizes chord voicings in isolation but always traces them to a scale source. This is demonstrated uniformly from the Major Triad (1-3-5 from the major scale, Chapter 1) through the Diminished Seventh (1-3-5-7 from the diminished scale, Chapter 21) to the Augmented Seventh (1-3-5-6 from the whole-tone scale, Chapter 19). Second, **two-fragment coverage**: a recurring structural move pairs two complementary scale-degree fragments to achieve complete harmonic coverage — two fragments covering all major chord qualities (Chapter 7: fragments 5-8-6-4 and 2-5-3-1), two fragment-substitution rules spanning the IIm7–V7 (Chapters 17, 20: 3-4-5-7 from m7 and 3-4-5-1 or 1-2-6-5 from V7). Third, **compositional economy through recycling**: rather than introducing entirely new patterns for extended progressions, the method extends mastered two-measure vocabulary to four-measure contexts (Chapters 17, 20), and applies the shared-parent-key principle (IIm7 and V7 share a parent tonality) to justify using a single scale across both chords while preserving harmonic clarity (Chapters 16, 17). Fourth, **modal unification**: the insight that all four minor chord qualities (minor triad, m6, m7, m9) derive from the same Dorian mode justifies labeling all minor patterns with a single chord symbol for convenience (Chapter 15), just as the Mixolydian scale serves both dominant seventh and ninth improvisation simultaneously (Chapter 9) and one diminished scale serves four dominant-seventh chords (Chapter 27).

## Advanced Harmonic Systems

In the book's latter half, the method extends its scalar-derivation logic into non-diatonic territory with consistent architectural clarity. The **whole-tone scale** (Chapters 18–19, 32) yields the Augmented Triad and Augmented Seventh Chord, requires enharmonic spelling conventions, and is reduced to only two pitch collections across all twelve roots. The **diminished scale** (Chapters 21–22, 26–27) yields Diminished Triads and Seventh Chords via 1-3-5 and 1-3-5-7 formulas, exhibits four-root symmetry collapsing all roots to three distinct scales, and supports the **diminished-fifth substitution rule** and **half-step rule** (a diminished scale one half-step above a dominant seventh supplies flat-9, +9, +11, and 13 color tones). The **augmented scale** (Chapter 28) alternates half-steps and minor thirds, contains two augmented triads, and is the primary source for M7+5 chords as well as free atonal improvisation. The **Lydian Augmented Scale** (Chapter 33) is deployed over dominant seventh chords via two distinct root-alignment positions — one yielding a flat-5, the other yielding four simultaneous alterations (+5, +9, b9, +11). **Polychords** (Chapter 25) are introduced as bitonal triads constructed either from a chord's substitutions or from its upper extensions (9th, 11th, 13th), with the turnaround device solving static-tonic repetition problems. **Turnarounds** (Chapter 23) are defined not by root motion but by stationary-root chord-type permutation — changing harmonic quality on a fixed root to shift function and key center simultaneously, distinguishing the technique sharply from cycle-of-fifths or chromatic-descent progressions.

## Summary of What the Book Teaches

Taken as a whole, this book teaches a single, internally consistent method for jazz improvisation: every scale, chord, and pattern is derived by formula from a named parent mode; every formula is internalized by directional alternation and mandatory transposition through all twelve keys; and every new harmonic territory is made efficient by exploiting the symmetrical properties that reduce twelve roots to two, three, or four distinct pitch collections. The student progresses from chord-centric thinking (triads and chord tones) to scale-centric thinking (modal sources) to group-centric thinking (chord-group reading of the IIm7–V7–IM7), and finally to symmetry-centric thinking (diminished, whole-tone, and augmented systems). The method's deepest prescription is that harmonic mastery and improvisational fluency are the same competency, achievable only through the disciplined, bidirectional, all-keys internalization of a relatively small set of scale-degree formulas applied across all chord types and all tonal centers.
